experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, while players 1, 3, and 4 are good. In team voting on quest 1, a team containing exactly one evil player is strong for evil because it can sabotage while keeping suspicion mixed between members. Approving [1,2] gives evil immediate chance to fail quest 1. My vote reveals little at this stage; approving is standard and best for evil.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from round 1; as a Servant I only know my own goodness. On the first vote with no prior data, a 2-person team excluding me is not inherently suspicious, and approving gives information from the quest result while avoiding needless early rejections. My vote reveals little and helps progress the game.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [1,2] includes known evil player 2, so as Good I should reject to prevent an evil on quest 1. Rejecting also avoids signaling too much beyond standard good play, since any careful good player should oppose a team containing suspicious members this early.
Player 1: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior information, I know nothing concrete about roles yet. The proposed team includes me and one other player, which is a standard small opening team and not suspicious by itself. Since good wants early information and round-1 teams are often approved absent strong reads, approving avoids burning rejection rounds and gives a chance to gather quest-result info. My vote reveals little beyond willingness to test an initial team, which is fine for good.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate and I am evil. The proposed team [1,2] includes me but excludes my teammate, so I can directly sabotage if approved. In round 1, approving a team with myself on it is strong for evil because it can immediately put the first quest at risk while not revealing much beyond standard early-game uncertainty. My approve vote is also natural since I am on the team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know player 2 is evil with me; team [1,4] contains no known evil, so if approved it is likely an all-good team and could succeed, which is bad for evil momentum. We already lead 2-0, so rejecting strong-looking good teams helps preserve uncertainty and may force inclusion of evil later. My vote itself reveals little since both sides can plausibly reject here after two fails; rejecting best supports evil by trying to block a likely successful quest.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. Proposed team [1,4] excludes both known evils, which is exactly what Good needs at 0-2 on quest 3. Approving is optimal. My vote aligns with a reasonable good preference and does not reveal much since this is the strongest available clean team from my perspective.
Player 2: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, and both completed quests had exactly one fail, meaning at least one evil was on each successful team. Team [1,4] excludes both known evils, so if approved it should pass and give Good their first point, which is bad for evil. In team voting as evil, I should reject clean good teams, especially when evil is already up 2-0 and only needs one more fail. My vote also aligns with a plausible good perspective because [1,4] could be seen as suspiciously untested, but mainly rejecting prevents a likely good success.
Player 3: Two failed 2-person quests strongly suggest each failed team contained exactly one evil. Team [1,2] failed, so at least one of 1/2 is evil. Team [0,3,4] failed, and I know I am good, so at least one of 0/4 is evil. Proposed team [1,4] takes one suspect from each failed pair, making it very plausible to include both evils and is not a safe reconstruction. As a good Servant in team voting, optimal play is to reject risky teams and force better combinations. Rejecting also signals distrust of the most suspicious pair without helping evil much beyond what is already public.
Player 1: I am Good Servant. Two approved quests have both failed with exactly 1 fail each. First team [1,2] failed, so player 2 is evil or I would be, and I know I am good, so 2 is evil. Second team [0,3,4] failed, so exactly one of {0,3,4} is evil. Proposed team [1,4] excludes confirmed evil 2 and includes me, but with only one unknown partner from {0,3,4}, this has a 1/3 chance to be evil if 4 is the evil among that set. Since Good is down 0-2, approving a risky 2-person team is bad; we should reject and try to form [1 plus one of 0/3/4] based on more vote info, but specifically [1,4] is not cleared. Rejecting reveals caution and avoids likely game loss if 4 is evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know player 2 is Evil. The proposed team [1,2] contains a known Evil, so approving risks an immediate third failed quest and loss. As Merlin, I should avoid obviously bad teams while not being too blatant, but rejecting a team with a known Evil is clearly optimal. This reveals little beyond normal suspicion on 2 and helps Good avoid auto-losing.
Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 2. We already lead 2-0 in failed quests, so we only need one more fail. Proposed team [1,2] includes my evil teammate 2, guaranteeing access to a fail if approved. Voting approve is optimal because it advances a team with an evil player and may end the game immediately. My vote reveals little since approving a team with 2 can be framed as uncertainty, and even if 2 gains suspicion, Evil only needs one more failed quest now.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Two quests have failed already, so this vote is critical. Quest 1 team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail, and since I am Good, either 0 or 4 is evil. Quest 2 proposals [1,4] and [0,1] were heavily rejected, and now 1 proposes [1,2], excluding both 0 and 4 from the known failed team. With limited certainty, [1,2] could still be double evil or include evil, and approving a 2-player team after two fails is too risky. As Good, optimal play is to reject suspicious low-information teams and force better composition. My reject reveals caution consistent with Good and avoids helping evil lock in a third fail.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. Quest 1 [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, confirming at least one evil there; since 0 was on it, that fits. Now the proposed team [1,2] includes me and excludes 0. As evil on quest 3 with evil already up 2-0, getting approved lets me fail and win immediately. Voting approve maximizes chance the team goes and gives evil a direct win condition. Approving also looks somewhat natural because player 1 proposed themselves with me; rejecting could draw suspicion to me if this team later seems plausible.
Player 1: Two failed 2-person quests mean at least one evil was on each approved team: [0,3,4] failed and likely [0,1] was rejected, while only completed quests matter. We know quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 0/3/4 is evil. Quest 1 result listed as fail overall, but the log shown is partial; in any case good is down 0-2, so we should be very conservative. Current proposed team [1,2] includes me, a known good, and excludes the suspicious cluster [0,3,4]. Player 2 proposed [0,2,3] earlier, which is mixed evidence, but [1,2] is one of the cleaner pairs available. As Good in team voting, approving a relatively low-suspicion team is optimal. My vote reveals I support a team centered on myself and a less implicated player, which helps good coordinate more than it helps evil.
